title: Handling High-Cardinality Data
concept: High-cardinality features have too many unique values for one-hot encoding, requiring alternatives like frequency encoding or target encoding.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: How does dropping one column from a one-hot encoded feature help the machine learning model?

options.text: It avoids the dummy variable trap by removing redundant, perfectly correlated columns
It makes the model run slower but with more accuracy
It converts the categorical features back into text labels

exercises.explain: Dropping one column avoids multi-collinearity (the dummy variable trap) without losing any predictive information.

cards.title: A map of every zip code
cards.body: Imagine a dataset with a zip_code column. There are over 40,000 zip codes in the US. One-hot encoding this would add 40,000 new columns to your dataset!

cards.title: High Cardinality
cards.body: Cardinality refers to the number of unique values in a column. A feature is high-cardinality when it contains hundreds or thousands of unique labels, making one-hot encoding impractical.

cards.title: Grouping small cities
cards.body: If you're asking tourists where they live, you don't need a separate category for every tiny village. You might group them by state, region, or simply use 'Other' for rare places.

cards.title: Target & Frequency encoding
cards.body: Instead of one-hot encoding, we can use frequency encoding (replacing each category with how often it appears) or target encoding (replacing it with the average target value for that category).

cards.title: Don't just delete it
cards.body: It is tempting to delete high-cardinality columns because they are messy. But variables like 'Zip Code' contain rich spatial and demographic patterns — you just need a better encoding!

right.text:
Replace with target average
'NY' → $500k house avg
Direct correlation to target

exercises.prompt: What does the term 'cardinality' mean in the context of database columns?

options.text: The number of unique values in that column
The total number of rows in the table
Whether a column contains decimals or integers

exercises.explain: Cardinality measures the uniqueness of values. High cardinality means many unique categories.

exercises.prompt: True or false: One-hot encoding is the best choice for columns like 'IP Address' or 'User ID'.
exercises.explain: False. IP Addresses have extremely high cardinality, and one-hot encoding would blow up the size of your dataset.

exercises.sentence: ___ encoding replaces each category with the average target value for that group.
exercises.explain: Target encoding uses the average outcome of the target variable for each category to represent that label.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a high-cardinality feature?

options.text: City name in a global airline dataset
Is the user logged in? (Yes, No)

exercises.explain: A global airline dataset will contain thousands of unique city names, making it high-cardinality.

exercises.prompt: You are building a fraud detection model and have a merchant_name column with 5,000 unique values. If you use target encoding to replace each merchant name with the historic percentage of fraudulent transactions at that merchant, what is the primary risk?

options.text: Overfitting and data leakage, since you're using target information inside your feature
Underfitting, because the model won't see any numbers
The merchant names will be deleted
The dataset will gain 5,000 new columns

exercises.explain: Correct! Target encoding uses the target variable to calculate the feature values, which can lead to severe data leakage and overfitting if not carefully smoothed. Excellent job completing Unit 7 on Feature Encoding!
